planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; queen wreath is one of the most spectacular flowering vines for zone 9-11 gardens, producing cascading racemes of violet-purple flowers in spring and sporadically throughout the year, with a main bloom flush of extraordinary beauty.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; provide strong trellis, fence, or arbor support — queen wreath becomes a large, heavy vine. Petrea volubilis (queen's wreath or purple wreath) is a Mexican and Central American tropical vine producing some of the most spectacular flower displays of any vine in subtropical horticulture — cascading, wisteria-like racemes 10–18 inches long densely packed with tiny, star-shaped flowers in an extraordinary violet-purple color in spring, with a secondary flush after summer rains. Each individual flower consists of a larger, calyx-like structure that outlasts the true petals, persisting as a pale-blue to lavender star shape after the purple petals drop — creating two overlapping color displays as the raceme matures. The rough, sandpaper-textured leaves (responsible for the common name 'sandpaper vine') are semi-evergreen. The vine climbs by twining and becomes very large and woody over time; mature plants require substantial support. In zone 9, it may be damaged by hard freezes but typically resprouts from the base. One of the most beautiful flowering vines for Gulf Coast trellises and fences.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Vines

planting.note: Plant container stock or rooted cuttings in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic, moderately fertile soil in part shade to full sun; flaming glorybower is a tropical West African vine that blooms most prolifically in bright, indirect light or part shade conditions in warm, humid climates.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; provide trellis or arbor support. Clerodendrum splendens (flaming glorybower) is a spectacular West African tropical vine producing dense, terminal clusters of brilliant scarlet-red, tubular flowers with prominent protruding stamens in large, rounded cymes that blaze against the dark, glossy, heart-shaped leaves — one of the most intensely colored flowering tropical vines available. Unlike the better-known bleeding heart vine (C. thomsoniae, with red and white flowers), C. splendens produces entirely scarlet flowers in even larger clusters and is a more vigorous climber. It blooms primarily in winter and spring in zone 10–12 — a welcome season when few tropical vines flower. The plant is an outstanding container plant and can be grown on a warm patio or sunroom in zone 9, brought outdoors in summer and protected in winter. Hummingbirds and butterflies are strongly attracted to the scarlet flower clusters. The vine roots easily from stem cuttings in warm, moist conditions.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Vines

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.5–6.5), fertile soil in part shade to full sun; yesterday today and tomorrow is a popular subtropical landscape shrub for zones 9–11, flowering most heavily in spring and sporadically year-round in frost-free conditions.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic to slightly acidic soil in part shade to full sun; slightly acidic soil (pH 5.5–6.5) produces best performance. Brunfelsia pauciflora (yesterday today and tomorrow or morning noon night) is a Brazilian tropical shrub famous for one of the most charming color-change performances of any flowering plant — new flowers open deep violet-purple ('yesterday'), fade to lavender the next day ('today'), and become nearly white by the third day ('tomorrow') — creating a three-toned tapestry of violet, lavender, and white flowers simultaneously visible on the plant at all times, against a backdrop of glossy, dark-green, somewhat wavy leaves. The flowers are sweetly fragrant, especially in the evening. The shrub is compact (3–6 feet), with a naturally tidy form that requires little pruning; it blooms most heavily in spring and may rebloom after summer rains. In zone 9, it typically survives mild winters with light frost damage, rejuvenating vigorously in spring; protect from hard freezes below 28°F. CAUTION: All parts of the plant are toxic if ingested; the berries are especially dangerous to children and pets.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Tropical Shrubs
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Subtropical Shrubs

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist to moderately dry, slightly acidic to neutral, moderately fertile soil in full sun to full shade; Turk's cap is one of the most shade-tolerant of all flowering shrubs and is outstanding for shaded garden spots where flowering shrubs rarely bloom — it produces its characteristic twisted red flowers prolifically in sun or shade.

directions: Plant in moderately fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist to moderately dry soil in full sun to full shade; Turk's cap tolerates a wide range of soil conditions and is virtually maintenance-free once established. Malvaviscus arboreus (Turk's cap or wax mallow) is one of the most beloved and useful native shrubs of south Texas and the Gulf Coast — a large, sprawling, multi-stemmed shrub native from Florida through Texas and deep into Mexico and Central America, producing a continuous show of small, unique, hibiscus-like flowers whose petals never fully open but instead remain spirally twisted around the central column, creating a distinctive, turban-shaped, bright-scarlet-red 'Turk's cap' flower from late summer through fall. It is among the very best plants for attracting hummingbirds and butterflies to the Gulf Coast garden. What distinguishes Turk's cap ecologically is its outstanding shade tolerance — it blooms prolifically under large live oaks, in deep woodland margins, and in full shade where almost no other flowering shrub succeeds. It is also drought-tolerant, deer-resistant in many areas, and nearly carefree. The small, red berries are edible (mildly sweet, jelly-like) and can be used to make preserves; they are also consumed by wildlife.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Shrubs
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Native Plants

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in very well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; Texas sacahuista is native to dry, rocky limestone plains and hillsides of central and west Texas and the Trans-Pecos and demands sharp drainage and lean soil — it will not tolerate wet, clay, or rich soil conditions.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, rocky to sandy, slightly alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; once established, Texas sacahuista requires no supplemental irrigation. Nolina texana (Texas sacahuista or Texas beargrass) is a native Texas perennial producing a dense, stemless rosette of very narrow (1/4-inch wide), fibrous, tough, grass-like leaves up to 3 feet long — creating a fountain-like mound of fine-textured, dark-green foliage that persists year-round. In late spring, mature plants send up a tall (3–5 foot), branching flower stalk bearing large panicles of tiny, white to cream flowers. Native Americans and early settlers used the tough, durable leaf fibers extensively for weaving baskets, mats, and sandals — sacahuista (the Mexican Spanish name) has roots in indigenous craft traditions of the Southwest. As a landscape plant, Texas sacahuista is valued for its extreme drought and heat tolerance, fine leaf texture, and year-round evergreen structure in hot, dry, alkaline gardens — an excellent companion to agaves, sotol, and native grasses in xeriscaping. It is also outstanding for erosion control on dry, rocky slopes. The flower stalks can be cut and used in dried arrangements.
sources: Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Native Plants
